topic: Someone finds out their best friend is moving across the country
raw_output:
[story 1]

The envelope sat on my desk like a declaration of war. It was stark white, addressed in a looping, overly cheerful script, and inside, the news was laid bare: Liam was moving to Seattle. My jaw tightened, a dull ache spreading through my temples. I picked up the letter, my thumb tracing the embossed seal as if it were contaminated. I didn't bother reading the details; the facts were already etched into the shape of my reaction. I pushed the paper away, the sound crisp in the quiet apartment. "Of course," I muttered to the empty room, the words tasting like ash. He always had to be the one chasing some grand, impossible horizon. It felt like a betrayal, a deliberate severing of our shared history, and I wanted to physically recoil from the very thought of him embracing a new life so far away.

[story 2]

I watched Sarah across the crowded coffee shop, her laughter bright and unburdened. Then, the email landed. A simple notification, a relocation confirmation. My hand, gripping my lukewarm mug, began to tremble almost imperceptibly. I stared at the screen, the words blurring into meaningless shapes. A slow, cold knot formed in my stomach, tightening with every passing second. I forced a casual sip of coffee, trying to keep my expression neutral, but I could feel the heat rising in my neck. When she finally looked up, her smile didn't falter, and that indifference was the sharpest thing of all. "So, Seattle," I managed, my voice sounding unnaturally flat. It was a performance, I knew, but the effort to sound unaffected was exhausting.

[story 3]

The glossy brochure for the new city was spread across the floor, its vibrant images mocking the muted reality of my current life. I kicked at the edges of the paper, a sharp, agitated motion. When I finally found the confirmation text, it was like a physical blow. My breath hitched, shallow and quick. I stood up abruptly, pacing the small living room three times before stopping, leaning against the wall, trying to steady the tremor in my hands. I looked at the window, the city lights below seeming distant and irrelevant. A tight, bitter taste coated my mouth. He was leaving, chasing some fantasy, and I was left here, watching the foundations of our friendship crumble into dust.
